title: ETF Expense Ratio Index 2026 — Westmount Fundamentals
description: Some popular ETFs charge 10x more than nearly identical alternatives. We ranked 86 funds to find out which ones are quietly overcharging you.

schemas.headline: ETF Expense Ratio Index 2026
schemas.description: Compare expense ratios of 86 major ETFs across all categories. Find the cheapest S&P 500, bond, international, and dividend ETFs.

mainEntity.name: What is the cheapest S&P 500 ETF?

acceptedAnswer.text: SPLG (SPDR Portfolio S&P 500 ETF) is the cheapest S&P 500 ETF with an expense ratio of just 0.02%. VOO and IVV are tied at 0.03%. SPY, the original and most liquid, charges 0.0945%.

Yes. On a $100,000 portfolio over 30 years, the difference between a 0.03% and a 0.50% expense ratio is approximately $14,000 in lost returns (assuming 7% annual growth). For passive index funds tracking the same benchmark, lower expense ratios directly translate to higher net returns.

mainEntity.name: VOO vs SPY which is better?

acceptedAnswer.text: Both track the S&P 500. VOO charges 0.03% vs SPY's 0.0945%. For buy-and-hold investors, VOO saves ~$65/year per $100,000 invested. SPY has tighter spreads and higher volume, making it preferred by active traders. For long-term holding, VOO is the better choice.

mainEntity.name: What is the average ETF expense ratio?

acceptedAnswer.text: Across our index of 86 major ETFs, the average expense ratio is 0.164% and the median is 0.09%. Broad-market index funds average around 0.03-0.05%, while thematic and actively managed ETFs average 0.35-0.75%. The industry trend is strongly toward lower fees.

mainEntity.name: Which ETF provider has the lowest fees?

acceptedAnswer.text: Schwab and Vanguard offer the lowest average expense ratios. Schwab averages 0.046% across 8 ETFs, while Vanguard averages 0.055% across 17 ETFs. SPDR's Portfolio line (SPLG, SPTM, SPYG, SPYV) competes at 0.02-0.04%, though their legacy products like SPY and GLD are pricier.
